skill.name: tool-selection-framework
skill.description: Build structured decision frameworks for selecting AI coding tools based on codebase size, task type, reasoning depth, context limits, budget, and time pressure.
skill.summary: Select Claude Code, Codex, or Gemini CLI with a structured task-fit framework.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many high-risk patterns, but review found no executable code, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration. The alerts are false positives from markdown code fences, AI tool names, and planning language, with one benign command example for counting lines of code.

risk_factor_evidence.assessment: The only confirmed external command is a documentation example that runs cloc to count project lines. It does not fetch remote code, modify files, access secrets, or execute user-controlled input.

low_findings.title: Benign External Command Example
low_findings.description: The skill includes a bash snippet showing cloc . --exclude-dir=node_modules,venv for measuring codebase size. This is a local read-only counting command used to support tool selection, not command execution logic inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly visible in a markdown bash example and has a clear local measurement purpose. It has no network endpoint, secret access, or dynamic input construction.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command detections point to markdown fences around pseudocode, workflow examples, and output templates. These blocks document decision logic and do not contain executable Ruby backtick calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced markdown examples in SKILL.md. No Ruby code, shell interpolation, or executable skill script is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections are false positives caused by words in metadata and AI tool names, especially Claude Code and codebase references. The skill contains no cryptographic functions or hash verification logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found planning prose and tool names at the cited locations, not MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar weak cryptographic usage. No executable crypto code exists in the single scanned file.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Planning Language
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance detections are false positives from words about exploration, refactoring, architecture, and codebase understanding. No scanning targets, network probes, host enumeration, or discovery commands are present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe developer workflow analysis and task classification. They do not contain network addresses, probing utilities, or commands that enumerate systems.

content.user_title: Choose the Right AI Coding Tool
content.value_statement: Teams waste time when they choose AI tools without matching context limits to the task. This skill provides a structured framework for selecting Claude Code, Codex, or Gemini CLI workflows.
actual_capabilities: Classifies tasks by codebase size, task type, reasoning depth, budget, time pressure, and user expertise.
Recommends Claude Code for focused implementation, deep reasoning, and time-sensitive development work.
Recommends Gemini CLI for broad exploration of large or unfamiliar codebases.
Designs multi-phase workflows that use exploration summaries before focused implementation.
Provides decision matrices, workflow templates, and example scenarios for tool selection.
limitations: Tool capability numbers may become outdated as vendors change context windows and pricing.
The skill does not execute analysis or measure a repository without user-provided commands or data.
Recommendations depend on accurate estimates of codebase size, task scope, and urgency.
It focuses on Claude Code and Gemini CLI, with only general guidance for future tools.

use_cases.title: Plan Tooling for a New Feature
use_cases.description: Select whether a scoped feature should stay in Claude Code or start with broader exploration.

use_cases.title: Onboard to a Large Codebase
use_cases.description: Create a two-phase workflow that maps architecture first and then implements focused changes.

use_cases.title: Standardize AI Workflow Decisions
use_cases.description: Give teams a shared decision matrix for context budget, cost, speed, and reasoning needs.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Tool for My Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner selection for a single task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the tool selection framework for this task. Codebase size: [lines]. Task: [task]. Time pressure: [level]. Budget: [level]. My familiarity: [level]. Recommend one workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Focused and Exploration Options
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate comparison across two tool choices.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether this work should use Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, or a multi-phase workflow. Explain the decision using codebase size, task type, reasoning depth, context budget, and urgency.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Phase Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for large or unfamiliar systems.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Gemini exploration to Claude Code implementation workflow for this project. Include phase goals, context to load, checkpoint output, and when to switch tools.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Team Decision Policy
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced standardization for repeated engineering work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn this tool selection framework into a team policy. Define thresholds, exceptions, review checkpoints, and examples for focused work, exploration, security analysis, and production incidents.

output_examples.input: A 30K-line Flask API needs one new endpoint and tests.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use Claude Code or Codex directly. The task is focused, the codebase is small, and broad exploration would add cost without clear value.

output_examples.input: A new engineer must understand authentication in an 800K-line legacy system before migration.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use a multi-phase workflow. Start with Gemini CLI for broad architecture mapping, then move to Claude Code for security-sensitive implementation.

output_examples.input: A production bug affects a known module in a 100K-line service.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use Claude Code immediately with selective context. Time pressure and a bounded scope make fast focused reasoning more important than full-system exploration.

best_practices: Measure or estimate codebase size before selecting a tool.
Use broad exploration only when the task requires system-wide understanding.
Create a short checkpoint before moving from exploration to implementation.
anti_patterns: Using a large-context tool for small focused changes without a clear need.
Forcing a focused tool to explore an unfamiliar monolith without summaries.
Ignoring urgency, cost, and user expertise when choosing a workflow.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides decision guidance and includes one optional cloc example for measuring repository size.

faq.question: When should I choose Claude Code?
faq.answer: Choose Claude Code for focused implementation, deep reasoning, smaller codebases, and time-sensitive fixes.

faq.question: When should I choose Gemini CLI?
faq.answer: Choose Gemini CLI when broad exploration of a large or unfamiliar codebase is the main need.

faq.question: Can I use this with Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The framework applies to Codex when comparing context needs, reasoning depth, and implementation scope.

faq.question: Does the framework replace engineering judgment?
faq.answer: No. It structures the decision, but users should update assumptions for current tool limits and project constraints.

faq.question: How should teams use the output?
faq.answer: Teams can record the recommendation in planning notes, architecture decisions, or task kickoff documents.
